title: The nature of business activity
summary: 9609 AS — factors of production, adding value, opportunity cost, and economic sectors.
learningObjectives: Define business activity and adding value.
Identify factors of production and their rewards.
Explain opportunity cost in business decisions.
Describe primary, secondary, and tertiary sectors.

simpleExplanation.title: Why businesses exist
simpleExplanation.summary: Businesses combine land, labour, capital, and enterprise to create goods and services worth more than the inputs — that gap is added value.
steps: Land, labour, capital, enterprise — the four factors of production.
Adding value: customers pay more than the cost of bought-in inputs.
Opportunity cost: the next-best option forgone when choosing a strategy.

flashcards.back: Land, labour, capital, enterprise — rewards: rent, wages, interest, profit.
Difference between selling price and cost of materials/components bought in.
Benefit of the best alternative given up — central to business choices.
Extracting raw materials — farming, fishing, mining.
Manufacturing and construction — transforms raw materials.
Organises other factors, takes risk — reward is profit (1.1.2).
Transformation process: inputs → process → outputs with value added.

sections.content: **The nature of business activity** (1.1.1) opens **9609 AS**. Use **applied examples** from the case — not textbook definitions alone.

sections.question: Coffee shop buys beans for $2 per cup served, sells drinks for $5. Explain adding value and name two factors of production used.
sections.solution:
**Adding value** = $5 − $2 = **$3 per cup** — covers **labour** (barista wages), **capital** (machine, rent), **enterprise** (owner's risk), and **profit**.

**Factors used:** **Land** (premises location), **labour** (staff), **capital** (espresso machine), **enterprise** (owner planning and risk).

**Opportunity cost:** Owner could have rented premises to another tenant — forgone rent is opportunity cost of running the shop.
